coredata.dc:title: Versatile carbon dots with superoxide dismutase-like nanozyme activity and red fluorescence for inflammatory bowel disease therapeutics

Yan, Xiangji

coredata.dc:description: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), including ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), is a chronic and recurrent disease that seriously affects the life quality of patients. Oxidative stress induced by abnormally high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the colon is the characteristic and pathogenic factor of IBD. Therefore, eliminating ROS in the inflamed colon is a promising approach for the treatment of IBD. This study employed a carbon dots (C-dots) nanozyme derived from glutathione and biotin via a solvothermal method to relieve IBD. We found that the resulting C-dots nanozyme owned both favorable superoxide dismutase (SOD) enzymatic activity and fluorescence capability. In vitro experiments showed that the C-dots could eliminate the excessive ROS to protect cells against oxidative stress and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. In IBD mice models (for both colitis and Crohn's), we found that administration of the C-dots with great biocompatibility could effectively alleviate the inflammation of the colon, including the reduction of the colon length, the damaged epithelium, the infiltration of inflammatory cells, and the overexpression of the pro-inflammatory cytokines. Moreover, the preferable fluorescence property of the C-dots could be used for in vivo imaging. Overall, this C-dots nanozyme, without appreciable systemic toxicity, represents a novel therapeutic approach for IBD and is expected to achieve long-term inflammatory remission.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of C-dots nanozyme with imaging and ROS scavenging properties as well as their therapeutics in IBD, including UC and CD. (A) Synthesis of the C-dots. The C-dots were synthesized through a solvothermal method based on biotin and GSH. The prepared C-dots could be used for ROS scavenging and imaging. (B) The applications of the C-dots in the treatment of IBD (UC and CD). The colonic epithelium was damaged in inflammatory conditions, and the ROS and pro-inflammatory factors were overproduced. After systemic injection, the C-dots entered the lamina propria of the colon, cleared the excessive ROS, and decreased the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, thus alleviating the inflammation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization and ROS scavenging capacity of the C-dots. (A) Graphical route of the synthesis of the C-dots. (B) The TEM image of the C-dots. Scale bar: 20 nm. The inset is the HR-TEM image and lattice of the C-dots. Scale bar: 2 nm. (C) The XPS results of the C-dots. (D) FT-IR spectrum of the C-dots. (E) Zeta potential of the C-dots. (F) The UV spectrum and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the C-dots (10 μg/mL). The inset is the images of the C-dots under bright and UV light. (G) The fluorescence spectra of the C-dots under different pH values. (H) The total anti-oxidation capacity of the C-dots of different concentrations. (I) The SOD-like activity of the C-dots of different concentrations. (J) The •OH scavenging ability of the C-dots (20 μg/mL). The inset is the image of the different reaction systems. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vitro anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects of the C-dots. (A) The diagram of the experiments. (B) Fluorescence images of RAW264.7 cells under different conditions after incubation with DCFH probe. Scale bar: 100 μm. (C) The statistics of green fluorescence in Figure B. (D) The flow cytometry results of the fluorescence in Figure B. (E) The cell viability of RAW264.7 cells in H2O2-treated inflammatory conditions with/without C-dots. (F) The cell viability of colon-26 cells in H2O2-treated inflammatory conditions with/without C-dots. (G) The mRNA expression of different pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β) in different conditions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vivo fluorescence imaging of the C-dots. (A) Images of fluorescence imaging in healthy mice after administration with 5 mg/kg C-dots at different times. (B) Fluorescence images of isolated organs of healthy mice after administration with 5 mg/kg C-dots at different times. H: Heart; Li: Liver; S: Spleen; Lu: Lung; K: Kidney. (C) The statistics of fluorescence intensity of isolated organs in Figure B. (n = 3) (D) The pharmacokinetic curve of the C-dots in healthy mice. (n = 3) (E) Images of fluorescence imaging both in healthy mice and two types of inflammatory models. (F) The statistics of fluorescence intensity of the major organs in Figure E. (n = 3) (G) The statistics of fluorescence intensity of the colons in Figure E. (n = 3).

The therapeutic effect of the C-dots in the colitis model. (A) Diagram of the C-dots in the treatment of UC mice. (B) Changes in body weight of mice. (C) The length of the colon. (D) Endoscopic images of mice in the different groups. (E) HE staining results. Scale bar: 200 μm. (F) Histological scores in different groups based on HE staining results in Figure E. (G) DHE staining images. Scale bar: 100 μm. (H) The mRNA expression of different pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The therapeutic effect of the C-dots in the CD model. (A) Diagram of the C-dots in the treatment of CD mice. (B) Body weight changes of mice. (C) Images of the colons. (D) The length of colons. (E) Endoscopic images of mice. (F) HE staining results. Scale bar: 100 μm. (G) Histological scores based on HE staining results in Figure F. (H) The mRNA expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-12). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Long-term biocompatibility of the C-dots. (A) Concentrations of blood routine parameters. (B) Concentrations of blood biochemistry parameters groups. (C) HE staining of the major organs. Scale bar: 100 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Versatile carbon dots with superoxide dismutase-like nanozyme activity and red fluorescence for inflammatory bowel disease therapeutics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), including ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), is a chronic and recurrent disease that seriously affects the life quality of patients. Oxidative stress induced by abnormally high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the colon is the characteristic and pathogenic factor of IBD. Therefore, eliminating ROS in the inflamed colon is a promising approach for the treatment of IBD. This study employed a carbon dots (C-dots) nanozyme derived from glutathione and biotin via a solvothermal method to relieve IBD. We found that the resulting C-dots nanozyme owned both favorable superoxide dismutase (SOD) enzymatic activity and fluorescence capability. In vitro experiments showed that the C-dots could eliminate the excessive ROS to protect cells against oxidative stress and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. In IBD mice models (for both colitis and Crohn's), we found that administration of the C-dots with great biocompatibility could effectively alleviate the inflammation of the colon, including the reduction of the colon length, the damaged epithelium, the infiltration of inflammatory cells, and the overexpression of the pro-inflammatory cytokines. Moreover, the preferable fluorescence property of the C-dots could be used for in vivo imaging. Overall, this C-dots nanozyme, without appreciable systemic toxicity, represents a novel therapeutic approach for IBD and is expected to achieve long-term inflammatory remission.

A carbon dot SOD nanozyme with red fluorescence exhibited exciting therapeutics in IBD models (both colitis and Crohn's).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), mainly consisting of ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), is a complex and incurable disease of the gastrointestinal tract [1,2]. For UC, inflammation is limited to the colon and mainly affects the mucous layer. While in CD, transmural inflammation often occurs, resulting in the invasion of any part of the digestive tract. IBD exhibited plaguing symptoms in patients, including abdominal pain, diarrhea, bloody stool, weight loss, etc. Clinical prescription medications have been applied to relieve symptoms, including aminosalicylic acid, glucocorticoids, and biological agents [3,4]. Nevertheless, the condition of IBD is still out of control due to the side effects of the current drugs [5–8]. Thus, the life quality of patients is seriously affected, which would cause a severe medical burden for society. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an innovative therapeutic strategy for IBD to realize the long-term remission of inflammation.

Although the pathogenesis is unclear, studies have found that reactive oxygen species (ROS) have participated in IBD. ROS, highly reactive oxygen metabolisms produced through the endogenous pathway and environmental stimulus in the living body, are important modulators during the progression of inflammation [9–11]. ROS mainly include superoxide anion (O2 •-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radical (•OH), etc. In normal conditions, the antioxidant system, consisting of endogenous antioxidant enzymes such as superoxide dismutase (SOD) and small molecules, maintains redox homeostasis. In contrast, excessive ROS is generated during the progression of inflammation, which exceeds the eradication capacity of the endogenous antioxidant system, thus causing oxidative stress [11,12]. As the overproduction of ROS induces the damage of lipids, protein, and DNA through targeting cysteine redox switches, the function of the colon and immune system could be disrupted, and the inflammation would be aggravated [10,13–16]. Previous reports have demonstrated that activated neutrophils and macrophages overproduced ROS in the colon both in UC and CD [17,18]. Moreover, the activity of the main anti-oxidase SOD and glutathione peroxidase (GPx) in the blood and mucosa was reduced [18,19]. Thus, supplementation with antioxidants would be promising in alleviating IBD [9,20]. Among them, natural enzyme supplementation would effectively alleviate IBD by converting toxic ROS to innoxious products. However, the deficiencies of the natural enzyme, such as instability, seriously limit their applications [21–23].

Nanozyme is a nanoparticle with enzyme-like activity by simulating the catalysis site of the natural enzyme or doping with essential elements in enzymatic reaction (such as metal ions and carbon elements). With superiority such as high stability, low cost, and ease of modification, nanozyme has attracted increasing attention in biomedical applications [24–27]. Up to now, various nanozymes with different enzymatic activities, including hydrolase, peroxidase, oxidase, and anti-oxidase, have been exploited. Among them, nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity has been widely used in biosensors [28–30]. In recent years, we have also witnessed the development of nanozymes with anti-oxidase enzymatic activity and their applications in the therapeutics of inflammation-related diseases [31–35]. For example, Prussian blue nanozyme with multienzyme-like activities could clear ROS including •OH, H2O2, and O2 •-, thus reducing the inflammation in colitis, neurodegeneration, liver injury, skin flaps, stroke, and so on [35–39]. Therefore, the nanozyme would be a promising therapy in IBD alleviation.

In this study, we introduced carbon dots based on glutathione (GSH) and biotin through a solvothermal method (Fig. 1 ), which exhibited good SOD enzymatic activity, hydroxyl radical scavenging ability, and fluorescence properties. The preferable ROS scavenging capability of the C-dots would be promising in IBD therapeutics through clearing excessive ROS at the inflammatory site and then remodeling the redox environment of the colon. Undoubtedly, in vitro experiments demonstrated that this nanozyme showed exciting anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory ability in lipopolysaccharide (LPS) induced inflammation. In IBD models (UC and CD mice), this nanozyme could effectively alleviate the inflammation of the colon by recovering the damaged epithelium and colon length, decreasing the expression of the pro-inflammatory factors. Moreover, this nanozyme could be used for in vivo imaging as well. Therefore, this nanozyme would provide a new therapeutic approach for IBD.

D-Biotin was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GSH and formamide were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), l-methionine (L-met), riboflavin, and LPS were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A superoxide dismutase assay kit with WST was purchased from Dojindo Molecular Technologies Co., Ltd. Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ferrous chloride (FeCl2·4H2O) was purchased from Tianjin bailens Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Total antioxidant capacity assay kit with ABTS method, ROS assay kit, DHE probe were obtained from Beyotime biotechnology. All chemical reagents were used when received without any further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images were obtained from an FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by a Nicolet 5700 instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi+. Zeta potential was measured by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSE. Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was recorded by SHIMADZU UV-2700 Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum was obtained from a HITACHI spectrophotometer (F-4700). In vivo imaging was recorded through a VISQUE® InVivo Smart-LF system.

The C-dots were synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method. In brief, 1.2 g of D-biotin and 1.2 g GSH were added into 40 mL formamide. Then the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave, followed by a reaction at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained products were dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) 3500Da) in deionized water for 7 d. Then the products were filtered with 0.22 μm filters followed by concentration and lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total antioxidant capacity of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The total antioxidant capacity of the C-dots was evaluated through the instrument of the commercial Total Antioxidant Capacity Assay Kit with the ABTS method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: O2 •- scavenging capability of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: NBT method: different concentrations of the C-dots were mixed with L-met (13 mM), NBT (0.05 mM), and riboflavin (20 μM) in PBS buffer (25 mM, pH 7.4). Then the absorbance of mixed solutions at 560 nm was continuously measured after LED irradiation for 10 s intervals [40].
WST method: this method was according to the instrument of the commercial colorimetric SOD assay kit (S311-10, Dojindo Molecular Technologies).

20 μg/mL C-dots were incubated with 1 mM Fe2+ and 5 mM H2O2 in PBS buffer (25 mM, pH 7.4) for 5 min. Then 0.25 mM MB was added followed by monitoring the absorbance between 300 and 800 nm in a multiple plate reader [41].

For the internalization in the normal cell, RAW264.7 and colon-26 cells were seeded in a glass slide and cultured overnight. Then the C-dots were incubated with cells for 8 h, followed by staining the cell skeleton and nucleus with FITC-actin and DAPI. The stained slides were observed under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (Olympus, Japan). The fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in cells was quantified by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) analysis (BD, U.S.). Concentration- and time-dependent cellular uptake of the C-dots were monitored through flow cytometry. As for the internalization in damaged cells, RAW264.7 and colon-26 cells were seeded in 24-well plates and cultured overnight. Then they were incubated with the C-dots and different concentrations of H2O2 for 8 h, followed by quantification through flow cytometry [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro ROS scavenging capacity of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: RAW 264.7 cells were seeded into a 12-well plate for 24 h in a 37 °C incubator followed by adding the C-dots of different concentrations for another 8 h. Then LPS (50 ng/μL) were incubated with cells for 12 h to induce inflammation. And DCFH-DA probe was used to evaluate the ROS levels through a fluorescence microscope and flow cytometry [43].

RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate overnight, followed by incubation with C-dots for 8 h H2O2 was added for 4 h to induce inflammation. Then 20 μL MTT was used to monitor the cell viability [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-inflammation of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: RAW 264.7 cells were seeded into a 6-well plate overnight, and then C-dots of different concentrations were further incubated for another 8 h. Next LPS (50 ng/μL) was used to induce inflammation. After 12 h, RNA was extracted by RNA isolation kit, followed by reverse transcription to cDNA. And then the cDNA was amplified through qPCR to evaluate the expression level of cytokines [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of the colitis model and Crohn's model
ce:section.ce:para: For the colitis model, C57BL/6 mice were continuously given drinking water including 2.5% dextran sulfate sodium (DSS) for 7 d [44].

For Crohn's model, C57BL/6 mice were pre-sensitized with 1% 2, 4, 6-trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid (TNBS) in 45% ethanol. After 7 d, C57 BL/6 mice were given 2.5% TNBS in 45% ethanol through the rectum [43].

5 mg/kg C-dots were injected intravenously into healthy C57BL/6 mice, and then in vivo imaging was conducted through VISQUE® InVivo Smart-LF system at different times (5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h) after injection. After in vivo imaging, blood samples, major organs, and the colons of the mice were collected. The blood samples were used to obtain the serum, then the fluorescence intensity in the serum was recorded to calculate the half-life of the C-dots. And the major organs and the colons were used for imaging to evaluate the distribution of the C-dots [42].

To compare the accumulation of C-dots in colons between healthy and inflammatory mice, the colitis model and Crohn's model were established. The same dosage of C-dots was administrated to healthy and two kinds of inflammatory mice. At 20 min after injection, the mice were sacrificed, and major organs and colons were collected and imaged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of the C-dots in colitis mice
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficacy of the C-dots in colitis, we constructed an acute colitis model. C57BL/6 mice were divided into five groups. The control group was given normal drinking water. The colitis mice were constructed by continuously drinking water including 2.5% DSS for 7 d, during which the C-dots and PBS were intravenously injected every other day. The stool, weight, and other states were observed and recorded every day. Before sacrifice, the colons were observed through the endoscope. On day 8, all mice were sacrificed. After sacrifice, the colons were collected and then used for hematoxylin & eosin (HE) staining, DHE staining, and qPCR [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of the C-dots in Crohn's mice
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficacy of C-dots in Crohn's disease, we constructed an acute Crohn's model. C57BL/6 mice were divided into four groups. The control group was given no treatment. The model mice were constructed by pre-sensitization with 1% TNBS in 45% ethanol. After 7 d, C57BL/6mice were given 2.5% TNBS in 45% ethanol through the rectum. The sham group was given 45% ethanol without TNBS. After the enema, C-dots and PBS were intravenously injected every day. The stool, weight, and other states were observed and recorded every day. Before sacrifice, the colons were observed through the endoscope. On day 12, all mice were sacrificed. After sacrifice, the colons were collected and then used for HE staining, and qPCR [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy C57BL/6 mice were intravenously administrated with PBS and C-dots of different concentrations (1/5/10 mg/kg) every other day three times. At 8 d and 30 d, the blood was obtained for blood routine and biochemical analysis. And the major organs were collected for HE staining [42].

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-tests were used to determine the statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001); a p-value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots were synthesized through a solvothermal method based on GSH and biotin (Fig. 2 A). The morphology of the obtained C-dots was characterized through TEM. As shown in Fig. 2B, the C-dots were nearly spherical with an average diameter of 3.1 ± 0.5 nm. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images (inset of Fig. 2B) showed the clear lattice fringes of C-dots with an average d-spacing of 0.21 nm, which was consistent with the previously reported (100) facet of graphite [45,46]. XPS and FT-IR measurements were further used to analyze the structure and composition of the C-dots. The results of XPS showed that there were four representative peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p at 284.8 eV, 400.09 eV, 531.44 eV, and 163.73 eV, respectively, indicating the as-prepared C-dots were composed of C, N, O, and S elements (Fig. 2C). Regarding the amount of each element, the C element accounted for the majority of all elements (64.2%), and the content of N, O, and S elements was 15.73%, 19.14%, and 0.93%, respectively. In high-resolution profiles of C1s (Fig. S1A), four peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV, 286.7 eV, and 287.9 eV were assigned to CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, CN/CO, respectively [47,48]. As for the N1s spectrum, three typical peaks at 398.6 eV, 399.9 eV, and 400.2 eV corresponding to pyridine-like N, pyrrolic-like N, and amino N, were fitted (Fig. S1B) [49]. FT-IR results showed a broad absorption band from 3000 to 3700 cm−1, which belonged to stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H. The peaks at 1398 cm−1 belonged to C–N. Moreover, other functional groups were CO at 1680 cm−1, CC/CN at 1596 cm−1, and C–O at 1158 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2D). Both XPS and FT-IR results demonstrated that there were abundant functional groups in the prepared C-dots. Furthermore, the zeta potential of the C-dots was −24.8 mV (Fig. 2E), indicating the colloidal stability of these C-dots.

Due to their excellent fluorescence property, C-dots have been widely used in fluorescence imaging [50–52]. To investigate the fluorescence imaging capability of the prepared C-dots, we evaluated their fluorescence property. As shown in Fig. 2F, the synthesized C-dot solution was green, and when irradiated under ultraviolet light, the C-dots would emit red fluorescence. UV–Vis absorption spectrum showed three main absorption bands at 200–300 nm, 350–450 nm, and 550–750 nm. The absorption band at 200–300 nm was attributed to π→π* transition of the aromatic CC bond, and the bands at 350–450 nm and 550–750 nm were assigned to π→π* and n→π* transitions of the aromatic π system containing the CO, CN, and CS bonds, respectively [53,54]. The emission spectrum was excitation wavelength-independent (Fig. S2A), and under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, the maximum emission peak was at 680 nm (Fig. 2F). In addition, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the C-dots was 11.76%. The optical performance of the C-dots at the emission wavelength of 680 nm made the C-dots suitable for fluorescence imaging both in vitro and in vivo. Considering the physiological environment, such as slightly acidic conditions and redox disruption of IBD, the fluorescence stability of the C-dots in solutions with different pH values and H2O2 was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2G, the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots at pH values from 5.5 to 7.5 remained almost unchanged. Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum of the C-dots was also stable in solutions with different H2O2 concentrations from 0 to 100 μg/mL (Fig. S2B). Considering the blood environment, the C-dots were incubated with fetal bovine serum (FBS) for 7 days. The results showed that the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots kept almost stable (Fig. S2C). In addition, the C-dots exhibited clear and no precipitates were observed during the 7 d (Fig. S2D). These results showed that the C-dots exhibited preferable bio-stability in FBS. These results demonstrated that these C-dots could be used for imaging in inflammatory sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS scavenging ability of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether the C-dots owned the antioxidant capacity, we measured their total antioxidant capability through a Total Antioxidant Capacity Assay Kit. In this method, 2, 2′- azino - bis (3- ethylbenzthiazoline - 6 - sulfonic acid) (ABTS), as a color agent, could be oxidized to green ABTS + under the oxidants, while ABTS + would be inhibited when antioxidants existed, and thus the antioxidant capacity could be obtained through calculation of the absorption of ABTS + at 734 nm. As shown in Fig. 2H, with the increasing concentration of the C-dots, the inhibition rate of ABTS + increased. When the concentration of the C-dots was 100 μg/mL, the inhibition rate reached above 60%, demonstrating the C-dots exhibited ROS scavenging capability. To further explore the ROS scavenging performance of the C-dots, the SOD-like activity of the C-dots was monitored through the nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) method. NBT, as the probe of O2 •-, would be reduced to blue methyl hydrazine, which exhibited the maximum absorption at 560 nm. SOD enzyme could scavenge O2 •-, thereby inhibiting the production of methyl hydrazine. The kinetic curves at 560 nm exhibited that the SOD-like activity of the C-dots was positively related to the concentrations of C-dots (Fig. S3). In addition, the SOD-like activity of the C-dots was examined by a total SOD activity detection kit based on WST. As shown in Fig. 2I, the O2 •- inhibition rate of the C-dots was also concentration-dependent, and 10 μg/mL C-dots could decompose almost all O2 •-. The enzymatic activity of the C-dots was calculated to be about 3472 U/mg based on the WST results. Through comparing the SOD-like enzymatic activity of the C-dots with nanozymes previously reported, we found that C-dots exhibited comparable or better SOD-like activity (Table S1), which is promising for the following therapeutics. Hydroxyl radical (•OH), from metal iron and H2O2, is another ROS and plays an important role in inflammation. Therefore, we next examined whether the C-dots could clear •OH. Methylene blue (MB) exhibits absorption peaks at 660 nm, which could be discolored by •OH oxidation produced through the Fenton reagent, thus, it could be used as •OH indicator. As shown in Fig. 2J, a single MB solution was blue and exhibited apparent absorption at 660 nm. When Fenton reagents were added into MB, the color of MB faded with almost no absorption at 660 nm, which was recovered after adding C-dots into this system. This result demonstrated that the C-dots were able to scavenge the •OH. Although the C-dots exhibited no or mild catalse and glutathione peroxidase (GPx) activity which catalyze the H2O2, H2O2 could easily penetrate the cell membrane, and then react with iron ions through Fenton reaction to generate more toxic free radicals, •OH [55]. Through scavenging the •OH by the C-dots, H2O2 could be indirectly cleared. Altogether, these results showed that the C-dots could scavenge the ROS, which is promising in the IBD therapeutics.

Internalization of drugs by the target cells is the premise for their function. Macrophage and intestinal epithelial cells play a vitally important role during IBD progression, so macrophage RAW264.7 cells and colon-26 cells were selected for evaluating the capability of cell uptake. After incubating the C-dots with the above two cell lines for a different time, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots was quantified through flow cytometry. As shown in Figs. S4A and S4C, the intensity increased with the extension of incubation time. The fluorescence intensity of the C-dots was also concentration-dependent (Figs. S4B and S4D). Fluorescence staining was further used to evaluate cell uptake capability. After incubation of the C-dots with two cell lines for 8 h, both colon-26 and RAW 264.7 cells exhibited strong red fluorescence compared with the control (Figs. S5A and S5C). And flow cytometry showed the same results (Figs. S5B and S5D). To evaluate the internalization of C-dots in the inflammatory environment, we treated RAW264.7 and colon-26 cells with different concentrations of H2O2 to mimic the inflammation. Undoubtedly, the fluorescence intensity in H2O2-treated groups was higher than that of the single C-dots-treated group (Figs. S6A and S6B), which may be attributed to the damage of the cell membrane at the inflammatory sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: During the progression of IBD, ROS is overproduced, and excessive ROS, in turn, aggravates the inflammation. Therefore, scavenging the excessive ROS would cut the following inflammation pathway, thus reducing the inflammation. Based on the exciting ROS scavenging results of the C-dots through clearing the O2 •- and •OH, we examined the anti-oxidative effects of the C-dots in RAW 264.7 cells and/or Colon-26 cells (Fig. 3 A). 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), a ROS probe, was employed to incubate RAW264.7 cells under different conditions. When ROS existed, DCFH-DA would be oxidized to fluorescent 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF), thus indicating the production of ROS. When incubated with normal RAW264.7 cells, almost no green fluorescence was observed. In contrast, LPS-treated cells exhibited obvious green fluorescence, indicating that LPS could induce ROS production. Moreover, pre-incubation RAW264.7 cells with the C-dots significantly decreased the green dots, showing that the C-dots could scavenge the ROS (Fig. 3B). Statistics of the green fluorescence exhibited the same results (Fig. 3C). The ROS scavenging capability of the C-dots was further quantified by flow cytometry (Fig. 3D). After treatment by LPS, DCF fluorescence intensity increased to 43.02%, while pre-incubation with C-dots reduced the fluorescence intensity of DCF. When the oxidative stress was decreased by clearing the excessive ROS, colonic cells would be protected, and the microenvironment of the colon was recovered, which is helpful for the inflammation resolution. Although superoxide anion free radical is the earliest form of reactive oxygen radical, H2O2 is easy to obtain and relatively stable. As an important reactive oxygen species, H2O2 can freely pass through cell membrane structure and react with iron ions in cells to form extremely cytotoxic hydroxyl radicals through Fenton reaction, causing cellular oxidative stress. Therefore, H2O2 was chosed widely to induce oxidative stress, and thus evaluated the anti-oxidation capability [55]. The C-dots against oxidative damage in both RAW264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells were further verified through 3-(4, 5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays. As shown in Fig. 3E, the cell viability of RAW 264.7 decreased to 43% after treatment with 500 μM H2O2, while pretreatment with the 1 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL C-dots increased the cell viability to 68%, 77%, respectively. The same results occurred in colon-26 cells (Fig. 3F). After incubation with 500 μM H2O2, the cell viability of colon-26 cells reduced from 100% to 13%. In contrast, the cell viability increased after pretreatment with the C-dots. In addition, we examined the effect of the C-dots in another normal cell, the NIH3T3 cell, and the same trend occurred as that of the above two cell lines (Fig. S7). These results demonstrated that the C-dots could protect the cells from oxidative damage.

Apart from excessive ROS, pro-inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-6 (IL-6), interleukin-1β (IL-1β) were overproduced through unregulated immune response during inflammation, further leading to a catastrophic inflammation cascade, thus causing the damage of tissue [56]. Thus, we evaluated the anti-inflammatory effect of the C-dots by monitoring the messenger RNA (mRNA) expression levels of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β in inflammation models induced by LPS. Compared with normal RAW264.7 cells, LPS treatment significantly increased the levels of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β by 23-, 48,000-, and 47-fold, respectively (Fig. 3G), indicating the successful establishment of in vitro inflammation models. Undoubtedly, the production of these cytokines was significantly reduced after pre-incubation with the C-dots. These results demonstrated that the C-dots could effectively alleviate the in vitro inflammation through anti-oxidation and anti-inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above favorable fluorescence property of the C-dots and their stability in inflammation-mimic conditions, we assumed that the C-dots could be used for in vivo imaging. To detect in vivo imaging capability of C-dots, healthy mice were administered 5 mg/kg C-dots intravenously. After intravenous injection, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in the abdomen increased with the prolongation of time and reached the maximum at 20 min (Fig. 4 A). To further monitor the distribution of the C-dots, the mice were sacrificed after in vivo imaging, then the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and colons were collected for imaging. As shown in Fig. 4B and C, the C-dots were mainly accumulated in the kidney, liver, lung, and colon. In addition, to analyze the metabolism of the C-dots in serum, blood samples of each mouse at different times were collected to measure the fluorescence intensity. The half-life was calculated to be 11.5 min, indicating that the C-dots could be quickly metabolized out of the body (Fig. 4D).

As the difference such as acidity and redox microenvironment in the colon existed between normal and IBD mice, we further compared the distribution between healthy mice and inflammatory mice (DSS-induced colitis model and TNBS-induced Crohn's model) (Fig. 4E–G). And there was almost no distribution difference in vital organs and colon between healthy mice and inflammatory mice, which demonstrated the inflammatory microenvironment did not influence the fluorescence property of the C-dots. Therefore, the fluorescence property of the C-dots could be used for in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of the C-dots in colitis mice
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the promising results of both in vitro anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidative effects, we would further examine the therapeutic effects in the colitis model (Fig. 5 A). All mice were sacrificed at 8 d, and the colons were collected for further analysis. The weight loss at days 5–7 demonstrated the successful establishment of the colitis model (Fig. 5B). In addition, the colon length of the DSS group was shortened, which further indicated the success of the inflammation model. In contrast, after treatment with C-dots, the colon length of mice was increased (Fig. 5C and Fig. S8A). Endoscopic evaluation plays an important role in the disease activity assessment, monitoring the disease progress, and thus becomes a consensus guideline in the IBD intervention [57]. To intuitively observe the lesion of the colons, the endoscope was conducted before the sacrifice of the mice. In the DSS group, ulcerations in the colon were observed (Fig. 5D). Furthermore, the colons were uneven, and the blood vessels were not clear compared with the healthy colons. While the colon damage in the 5 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg C-dots-treated group was reduced. HE staining was further conducted to analyze the structure and pathological changes of colon tissues. As shown in Fig. 5E, compared with the colon of healthy mice, the loss of crypts, destruction of goblet cells, and inflammatory cell infiltration were observed in the DSS group, while these symptoms were alleviated in the C-dots-treated groups. The statistical result of HE staining showed the same results (Fig. 5F). As the C-dots exhibited SOD-like activity, we monitored the O2 •- levels in the colon through an O2 •- probe, dihydroethidium (DHE). DHE could be dehydrogenized by O2 •- followed by the production of ethidium, and ethidium could emit red fluorescence when binding to DNA or RNA. The intensity of red fluorescence is proportional to the amount of O2 •-. In the healthy colon, red fluorescence was almost invisible, while strong fluorescence was observed in the inflammatory colon (Fig. 5G). As expected, the intensity of red fluorescence reduced after administration with C-dots. Myeloperoxidase (MPO), a marker of inflammation [58], could monitor the inflammatory process. In the DSS group, the amount of MPO was increased, indicating the occurrence of colitis (Fig. S8B), while it was decreased in the C-dots-pretreatment groups. During the progression of IBD, the expression of pro-inflammatory factors was increased. To evaluate the alleviation of inflammation, we further detected the expression of cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12 in the colon. As shown in Fig. 5H, TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12 levels significantly increased after DSS induction, which was reduced after C-dots treatment. These results showed that C-dots exerted satisfactory effects in the alleviation of colitis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of the C-dots in Crohn's mice
ce:section.ce:para: IBD mainly consists of UC and CD. To further validate the effect of the C-dots on Crohn's disease, the mice were grouped into four groups: control group (healthy mice), sham group, TNBS group, and TNBS + C-dots group (Fig. 6 A). The treatment group was intravenously administrated with 5 mg/kg C-dots every day for 3 days. Then mice were sacrificed on day 12. The weight of mice reduced at day 9, indicating the onset of the inflammation (Fig. 6B). Macroscopic observation through the endoscope showed that the colon in the TNBS-treated group exhibited obvious lesions with an unsmooth colon, which was mitigated by the C-dots (Fig. 6E). Meanwhile, the colon length after TNBS induction reduced, and the content in the colon was unshaped, while after treatment with the C-dots, these parameters were alleviated (Fig. 6C and D). HE staining was further employed to evaluate the colons from the microscopic level. The results showed that the epithelium was disrupted, the crypt disappeared, and the inflammatory cells were infiltrated (Fig. 6F). In contrast, colon structure was recovered, and immune infiltration was reduced after C-dots administration. The histological scores showed the same results (Fig. 6G). Next, we examined the expression level of cytokines, and the results showed that TNBS induced the increase of the cytokines, including TNF-α and IL-12. In contrast, these factors significantly decreased after the administration of the C-dots (Fig. 6H). These results demonstrated that the C-dots were also effective in the relief of Crohn's disease.

The safety of any drugs is the first consideration for their transformation to clinical applications [59,60]. The hemolytic toxicity ot the C-dots was firstly measured. The results showed that the hemolysis ratio of the red blood cells was below 10%, even at the concentration of 3 mg/mL (Fig. S9), which demonstrated the C-dots could be used for the further intravenous injection. In vivo biocompatibility was then conducted in healthy mice. Healthy mice were intravenously injected with PBS and three different concentrations of the C-dots, respectively. And after short-term (7 days) and long-term (30 days) administration, the blood samples and major organs of mice were collected for blood routine examination, blood biochemical analysis, and HE staining. As shown in Fig. 7 A, blood routine parameters including white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), granulocyte (Gran#), lymphocytes (Lymph#), platelet count (PLT), and hemoglobin (HGB) in control groups (PBS treated) and C-dots-treated groups were all in the normal range (the normal range of WBC, RBC, Gran#, Lymph#, PLT, and HGB was 0.6–6.8 × 109/L, 6.36–9.42 × 1012/L, 0.1–1.8 × 109/L, 0.7–5.7 × 109/L, 490–1590 × 109/L, 110–143 g/L, respectively) during 30 d. In addition, serum biochemical indicators such as kidney function (alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST)), renal function (blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CREA)), and myocardial enzymes creatine kinase (CK) exhibited no significant difference in the control group and C-dots-treated groups, indicating there was almost no apparent blood toxicity of the C-dots (Fig. 7B). In HE staining results, no structure and pathological changes in C-dots-treated mice were observed (Fig. 7C). Biocompatibility during the short term was the same as that of the long term (Fig. S10). All demonstrated that the C-dots exhibited excellent biocompatibility.

In summary, a C-dots nanozyme based on GSH and biotin was prepared. The as-synthesized C-dots exhibited considerable SOD enzymatic activity and •OH scavenging ability to eliminate ROS, and fluorescence property for in vivo imaging. The C-dots nanozyme could not only eliminate the excessive ROS and protect cells against oxidative stress but also alleviate the inflammation in IBD mice effectively. Without appreciable systemic toxicity, this C-dots nanozyme represents a novel therapeutic approach for IBD and is expected to achieve long-term inflammatory remission.
All the other data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its supplementary material files and from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
